docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This rulebook describes a d20 system designed for dark fantasy settings. It is mechanically self-contained but assumes a world with the following properties:

  • The capacity for power is cosmic in origin and unevenly distributed at birth
  • That capacity is innate and fixed — it can be measured, degraded, but not trained into existence
  • Institutional religion has political interest in controlling those who possess it
  • Tragedy emerges from known prices paid, not from betrayal or corrupted wishes

What the cosmology calls this capacity, which god sanctions it, and what the Church looks like are yours to decide. The mechanics are indifferent to those answers.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
Narrative accuracy over abstraction. Combat mechanics reflect how medieval violence actually worked — accumulated degradation, decisive finishing strikes, precise bypasses of armour. Numbers should feel like something.

Fixed ceilings, meaningful choices within them. A character's innate capacity sets their ceiling. Everything else is how they spend what they have.

No moral champions. All characters — player and non-player — are morally grey. The system does not reward alignment. Pacts exist, and their tragedy comes from knowing the price and paying it anyway.

Portable core, honest defaults. The system ships with a medievalpunk setting as its default expression. Subclass names, institutional names, and cosmological flavour can be reskinned. The one hard requirement is a fiction that explains why some individuals have class potential and others do not.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Combat — Vitality, Wounds, hit locations, criticals
  • Kai & Mana — Innate capacity, mana pools, recovery, overdraw
  • Magic — Schools, spell levels, costs, the Free Casting feat chain
  • Classes — Class overview, subclass system, kai requirements
  • Pacts — Single-use power, known prices, karmic mechanics

docs.title: What a class is
docs.text:
A class is a calling, not a profession. It requires sufficient kai to manifest — without innate capacity, techniques can be learned but no class levels, bonuses, or kai-driven features are accessible. The vast majority of the population has no class.

Class-bearers are treated by institutional religion as assets first and people second. This is not flavour — it has mechanical consequences in social encounters, legal standing, and the attention of the Church's enforcement arms.

docs.title: Multiclassing
docs.text:
Multiclass characters do not exist under normal circumstances. A character has one class. Subclasses allow meaningful specialisation within that class.

Extraordinary circumstances — divine intervention, exposure to a cosmic event, a sufficiently costly pact — may produce a second class. These cases are narrative events adjudicated by the GM, not character build options.

docs.title: The four classes
docs.text:
Class Core drive Mana use Fighter Martial mastery, physical dominance Subclass-dependent only Healer Restoration, support, kai channelling Active; subclass-dependent Spellcaster School-based magic, kai shaping Active; central to the class Rogue Precision, deception, opportunity Subclass-dependent only

Fighters and Rogues do not spend mana as a baseline class mechanic. Their kai-limited features (precision techniques, favoured enemy counts, channel capacities) are bounded by kai tier but do not draw from a mana pool unless a specific subclass introduces active resource expenditure.

docs.title: Subclasses
docs.text:
A character may acquire subclasses during levels 4–13, gaining one subclass level per level in that range (10 subclass levels total). Subclasses are chosen by the player.

A character has one main class and may have multiple subclasses, provided all subclasses are compatible with the main class. A Fighter may be a Paladin/Berserker. A Fighter may not simultaneously be a Healer.

Subclass features are kai-limited in the same way as main class features.

docs.title: Fighter subclasses
docs.text:
Subclass Notes Berserker Rage-driven combat; offensive focus Paladin Faith-driven combat; defensive and support features Inquisitor Intelligence-based fighter; perception and interrogation features. Reskinnable — a secular equivalent exists under different names in non-Church contexts. Ranger Favoured enemy system; kai limits enemy count

Placeholder

Full fighter subclass list to be expanded. Inquisitor feature set to be detailed.

docs.title: Healer subclasses
docs.text:
Placeholder

Healer subclasses to be developed.

docs.title: Spellcaster subclasses
docs.text: Subclass Notes Generalist All schools; no sacrifice ring Specialist One school; sacrifice ring (see Magic) Battle Mage Spellcasting + martial hybrid Artificer-adjacent Imbuing and crafting focus

docs.title: Rogue subclasses
docs.text:
Placeholder

Rogue subclasses to be developed. Assassin subclass confirmed — stealth critical mechanic is the defining feature (see Combat).

docs.title: Kai requirements by class
docs.text:
Minimum kai to manifest a class is 1,000. No player character operates near this threshold — the practical floor for play is Forge tier (4,000+).

The kai tier sets the level ceiling for all class and subclass features. A Forge-tier character (4,000–4,999) can reach approximately level 17 regardless of class. A higher-tier character reaches further; a lower-tier character hits their ceiling earlier.

Placeholder

Exact kai-to-level ceiling mapping to be finalised. Current reference values are in Kai & Mana.

docs.title: Imbued artefacts
docs.text:
Spellcasters can imbue objects with kai, producing artefacts that allow non-class-bearers to use spell effects. These are wielded by common people who want to play the hero — often without the ending they would wish for.

Imbued artefacts are single-use or charge-limited. They do not grant a class. They do not respond to the wielder's kai (they have none). They behave exactly as the spellcaster who made them intended — no more, no less.

Placeholder

Artefact creation rules, charge mechanics, and cost to the creating spellcaster to be developed.

docs.title: Durability: two layers
docs.text: Combat damage operates across two distinct layers. These are not interchangeable — they represent fundamentally different things.

docs.title: Vitality
docs.text:
Stamina, adrenaline, near-misses, and the absorption provided by armour and positioning. Vitality depletes freely in combat with no fictional weight attached — the character is not yet being cut. It recovers relatively quickly outside of combat.

Vitality maximum is derived from Constitution and class.

docs.title: Wounds
docs.text:
The actual body. A fixed, low number (Constitution ÷ 4, minimum 1) representing how much real anatomical damage a character can sustain before death or permanent incapacitation. When Vitality reaches zero, overflow damage threatens Wounds directly.

Wounds do not recover quickly. Minor wounds require rest and treatment. Serious and Grievous wounds require a surgeon, not a healer — they sit outside the reach of magic.

docs.title: Damage dice
docs.text:
Every weapon has two dice:

Base die — the force of the hit. Scales with weapon, not character level. A dagger is d4; a greatsword is d10. A character's power at higher levels comes from more Vitality and better defences, not from absorbing more stab wounds.

Injury die — rolled only when a hit reaches Wounds. Always d6, determining wound severity regardless of weapon:

Injury die Severity 1–2 Minor 3–4 Serious 5–6 Grievous

docs.title: Hit locations
docs.text:
When a Wound is inflicted, roll or choose a hit location. The location is determined narratively where possible — a prone opponent is far more likely to take a head wound than a standing one.

d6 Location Condition 1 Head Concussion; perception impaired 2 Throat / neck Silenced; bleeding 3 Chest Stamina penalty; broken rib risk 4 Abdomen Winded; infection risk 5 Arm (weapon or shield) Disarm risk; attack penalty 6 Leg Movement halved; fall risk

A staggered or prone opponent shifts the location roll — the GM may reroll or choose, biasing toward head and neck. This reflects the historical record: killing blows in medieval combat were disproportionately delivered to downed opponents, targeting exposed anatomy.

docs.title: Wound severity
docs.text:
Wounds are not just hit point reductions. Each wound imposes a condition lasting until properly treated.

Severity Mechanical effect Treatment Minor Disadvantage on one relevant action type Rest + basic treatment Serious Ongoing Vitality loss (1 per round or per scene); hard penalty Physician + time Grievous Immediate stabilisation check or unconscious; permanent scar / impairment if untreated Surgeon + extended recovery

Multiple simultaneous wounds stack their conditions. A Grievous leg wound means the character is dragging a ruined leg — this is not abstracted away.

docs.title: Armour
docs.text:
Armour reduces injury severity rather than raw damage. A hit that reaches Wounds against a heavily armoured opponent rolls the injury die at disadvantage (take the lower result). This reflects the historical reality: armour doesn't stop force, it redistributes and reduces its anatomical consequence.

Armour tiers:

Tier Example Injury die 0 Unarmoured Normal roll 1 Padded / leather Normal roll 2 Mail Roll twice, take lower 3 Plate Roll twice, take lower; Minor on 1–4 4 Full plate Roll twice, take lower; Grievous only on 6

docs.title: Critical hits
docs.text: A critical hit bypasses Vitality entirely — the attack finds a gap in the defence and strikes anatomy directly.

docs.title: Critical Threshold (CT)
docs.text:
Every successful attack has a CT the attacker must also meet or beat:

CT = 15 + Defender Armour Tier − Attacker Precision Bonus

Precision is weapon-gated. A stiletto, estoc, or bodkin-tipped arrow can have high Precision. A greatsword cannot. This encodes the historical logic: armour is bypassed with the right tool, not raw strength.

Precision bonus is approximately −1 per 3 levels, capped by weapon type.

docs.title: The assassin exception
docs.text:
If the attacker is undetected at the moment of the strike, CT is replaced by:

CT = 10 − Attacker Precision Bonus

A skilled assassin with Precision +4 needs a 6 or better. This is their one window. Detection before the strike reverts CT to the standard formula.

docs.title: Critical effect
docs.text:
1. Bypass Vitality entirely
  2. Roll the injury die twice, apply the worse result
  3. Injury die is automatically d6 regardless of weapon base die

The double roll is not a guaranteed Grievous — two Minor results in different locations may be more debilitating than one Serious, depending on conditions.

docs.title: Historical note
docs.text:
Skeletal analysis of medieval mass graves (Towton 1461, Visby 1361) consistently shows multiple distinct wounds per combatant, many individually survivable. The killing blow is typically the last in a sequence — delivered to someone already incapacitated, often to the head or neck. Instant kills from a single torso strike were rare even among unarmoured combatants, and near-impossible against plate.

This system reflects that pattern. Vitality represents the degradation sequence. Wounds represent the moment it breaks through.

docs.title: What kai is
docs.text:
Kai is the innate capacity for class-level ability. In the default setting it originates from stellar radiation — exploding stars emit kai, and some individuals absorb it at a formative stage rather than letting it pass through like background radiation. It leaves physical residue, can be measured with instruments, and does not grant miracles.

The fictional explanation for kai's origin is reskinnable. The mechanical requirement is not: some individuals have it, most do not, and the amount is fixed at the point of awakening. It cannot be trained into existence.

Kai is the depth of the well. Mana is the water.

docs.title: Kai tiers
docs.text:
Range Tier Max level Profile 1–999 Inert No class The vast majority of the population. Kai is present but functionally irrelevant. ~95% of people. 1,000–1,999 Spark ~5 Detectable. Enough to manifest a class, barely. These individuals are registered and monitored. 2,000–2,999 Ember ~8 Minor local talent. A meaningful career is possible — guild enforcer, village healer, court artificer. 3,000–3,999 Flame ~13 Regional renown possible. The Church takes these individuals seriously. 4,000–4,999 Forge ~17 Standard player character range. Acts that become local legend. 5,000–5,999 Blaze ~20 Standard hero tier. A full campaign arc. Named in chronicles; remembered for generations. 6,000–6,999 Pyre ~20+ Rare enough that most people never meet one. Changes the outcomes of wars. 7,000–7,999 Inferno ~20+ A handful per generation, globally. Destabilises political structures. 8,000–8,999 Conflagration ~20+ Generational hero. Their awakening may have astronomical correlates. 10,000+ Stellar Uncapped Only a handful in recorded history. The Church has no doctrine for them — only silence.

No player character begins below Forge (4,000). The 1,000 class threshold is real but no one at the table operates near it.

docs.title: Intra-tier differences
docs.text:
Within a tier, the exact kai value produces small differences felt at character creation and in non-combat play only.

Each tier is divided into three bands:

Band Position Effect Low x000–x299 Base skill points for tier Mid x300–x699 +2 bonus skill points at creation High x700–x999 +2 bonus skill points + +1 to one class-relevant skill check category

The +1 skill check bonus is chosen from a short list tied to the character's class. It is never a free +1 to any skill.

docs.title: Choosing a kai value
docs.text: Players choose a tier appropriate to the campaign, then either roll (1d10 × 100 + 1d100 − 1) or pick a number within the tier. The number may be deferred — fixed narratively when it first matters, such as when the Church measures the character or an artificer reads their residue.

docs.title: Mana
docs.text:
Mana is the spendable resource for all kai-driven abilities across all classes. It is not exclusive to spellcasters — a fighter's precision techniques, a ranger's favoured enemy count, a healer's channel capacity all draw from the same pool where applicable.

Class variation

Not all classes use mana as an active resource. Fighters and rogues spend mana only through specific subclasses. Base class features for these classes are kai-limited but not mana-gated.

docs.title: Mana pool
docs.text:
Max mana = (10 × caster level) + (Intelligence modifier × caster level)

A level 10 character with Intelligence 18 (+4 modifier) has a mana pool of 140.

Intelligence therefore serves double duty: it gates maximum spell level (see Magic) and amplifies the mana pool. It is the primary stat for spellcasters without being the only one that matters.

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
Mana recovers with time only. There are no shortcuts in the base rules — no meditation, no potions, no ley line tapping. A wounded character on forced march still recovers, just more slowly.

Recovery rate scales with kai depth. A Blaze-tier character recovers meaningfully overnight. A Forge-tier character may need two or three days to feel full after serious expenditure.

GM's discretion

External mana sources (potions, ley lines, rituals) are setting-specific and not assumed by the base rules. GMs introducing them should treat them as significant and rare.

docs.title: Overdraw
docs.text:
A character may spend past zero mana. The threshold is perceptible — they feel it before they cross it. Crossing it is a deliberate choice.

Overdraw damage lands on the kai layer, not the wound system. It is not physical trauma. Healers, surgeons, and medicine cannot touch it.

Each overdraw event permanently reduces the kai ceiling by a small amount, which in turn slightly shrinks the mana maximum and may reduce kai-limited feature counts. Repeated overdraw is a slow self-erasure — a spellcaster who burns out habitually is gradually becoming someone without a class.

Overdraw damage does not recover under normal circumstances. Extraordinary narrative intervention — potentially including a pact — may be the only recourse.

Pact intersection

A spellcaster at zero mana facing certain death is exactly the conditions under which pact granters are drawn. The price of one last overdraw paid permanently in kai is structurally coherent with the pact system. See Pacts.

docs.title: Kai degradation reference
docs.text:
Overdraw events Approximate kai loss Practical effect 1 ~1–2% of tier Barely perceptible mana reduction 3–5 ~5–8% of tier Noticeable pool shrinkage 10+ ~15–25% of tier Possible tier boundary risk; feature loss Catastrophic Tier drop Effectively losing a class level ceiling

Placeholder

Exact degradation values per overdraw event are to be finalised during playtesting.

docs.title: Schools of magic
docs.text:
All spellcasters have access to all eight schools from level 1. There is no gating by level or study in the base rules — the ceiling is set by Intelligence and mana pool, not by school access.

The eight schools are:

School Domain Abjuration Protection, warding, counterspell Conjuration Summoning, teleportation, creation Divination Perception, foresight, information Enchantment Mind, compulsion, emotion Evocation Raw force, elemental energy, damage Illusion Deception, sensory manipulation, misdirection Necromancy Death, undeath, life force Transmutation Physical alteration, transformation, enhancement

Placeholder

Spell lists per school to be developed separately.

docs.title: Spell levels
docs.text:
Spells range from cantrips to level 9. Access to higher spell levels is gated by Intelligence, not caster level. Caster level determines the size of the mana pool; Intelligence determines how high you can reach into it.

Intelligence Max spell level 10–11 1 12–13 2 14–15 3 16–17 5 18–19 7 20–21 8 22+ 9

Levels 4 and 6 are never a stable ceiling — they are only accessible in transit between Intelligence thresholds. A high-Intelligence low-level caster can reach powerful spells but has the mana pool to cast very few of them.

docs.title: Mana costs
docs.text:
Spell costs follow the Fibonacci sequence. This produces natural exponential growth without arbitrary inflation, and the values map cleanly onto the spell level range.

Spell level Mana cost Cantrip 1 1 1 2 2 3 3 4 5 5 8 6 13 7 21 8 34 9 55

At a mana pool of 140 (level 10, Intelligence 18), a level 9 spell consumes 39% of total mana. A caster gets roughly two before entering dangerous territory. Level 9 spells are events, not options.

docs.title: The Free Casting feat chain
docs.text:
Cantrips and level 1 spells both cost 1 mana — the Fibonacci sequence begins with two 1s. The Free Casting feat chain represents mastery so complete that the lower tiers of magic cost nothing.

Each tier of the chain is a separate feat requiring the previous tier as a prerequisite.

Feat Effect Free Casting I Cantrips cost 0 mana Free Casting II Level 1 spells cost 0 mana · 1/day cast a level 1 spell at level 2 effect for free Free Casting III Level 2 spells cost 0 mana · 1/day cast a level 2 spell at level 3 effect for free

The daily metamagic push at each tier boundary is not a cost reduction — it signals that the caster has so thoroughly internalised that level of magic that they can push it beyond its natural ceiling once per day without expenditure.

Placeholder

The chain as written covers tiers I–III. Extension to higher tiers (IV+) to be determined during playtesting — the pattern holds but balance at high levels needs verification.

docs.title: The school sacrifice ring (Specialist subclass)
docs.text: The Specialist subclass organises the eight schools into a ring, with each school having a direct opposite across the ring. Sacrificing access to schools on either side of your chosen school unlocks deeper feature tiers within it.

docs.title: Ring arrangement
docs.text:
Evocation — Transmutation — Conjuration — Enchantment
    |                                           |
Abjuration — Necromancy  — Divination  — Illusion

Opposition pairs (directly across the ring):

School Opposite Evocation Illusion Transmutation Divination Conjuration Necromancy Enchantment Abjuration

docs.title: Sacrifice mechanics
docs.text:
Sacrifices peel symmetrically outward from the chosen school in both directions simultaneously. You cannot sacrifice one neighbour without the other.

Taking an Evocation specialist as an example:

Sacrifice tier Schools lost Feature unlocked 0 (none) — Tier 1 (base specialist) 1 Transmutation + Abjuration Tier 2 2 Conjuration + Enchantment Tier 3 3 Divination + Illusion Tier 4 4 Illusion (opposite) Tier 5 (savant)

Sacrificed schools are removed from the spell list entirely. The mana that would have gone to them does not transfer — the well simply has fewer channels.

Sacrifices are made at subclass selection (level 4) and can be deepened on subsequent levels. They are never reversed.

docs.title: Specialist and mana costs
docs.text:
Spells from the chosen school cost −20% mana per sacrifice tier, rounded down, minimum 1. A tier 4 specialist casts their school's spells at 20% of base cost.

Sacrifice tier Cost multiplier 0 100% 1 80% 2 60% 3 40% 4 20%

Placeholder

Feature tier contents (what each tier unlocks per school, across all 8 schools) to be developed separately. Five tiers × eight schools = 40 feature blocks.

docs.title: Spellcaster subclasses summary
docs.text:
Subclass Focus Generalist Access to all schools; no sacrifice ring; shallower ceiling Specialist Deep in one school via sacrifice ring Battle Mage Spellcasting + martial hybrid Artificer-adjacent Imbuing and crafting focus

Placeholder

Full subclass writeups to be developed. Generalist ceiling relative to Specialist to be balanced during playtesting. Battle Mage interaction with sacrifice ring to be determined.

docs.title: What a pact is
docs.text:
A pact is a single-use, supernaturally binding agreement between a person and an entity drawn to their despair. It functions mechanically like a powerful one-use feat. It functions narratively like a prayer answered by something that was already listening.

Pacts are not a class feature. Anyone can make one — class-bearer or not. The only requirement is sufficient desperation.

docs.title: How pacts work
docs.text:
Pact granters are drawn to despair. They do not seek out the powerful or the worthy. They find people at the end of their options.

The terms of a pact are always completely clear to both parties at the moment of agreement. There are no corrupted wishes. No malicious interpretations. No small print. If you agree to survive the battle at the cost of your companions' lives, you understand exactly what you are agreeing to. The tragedy of pacts does not come from being cheated — it comes from knowing the price and paying it anyway.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Every pact has:

  • The offering — what the petitioner receives. Always unambiguous.
  • The price — what the petitioner pays. Always unambiguous. May be immediate or ongoing.
  • The binding — once agreed, the pact cannot be undone. Attempts to cheat it do not produce loopholes. They produce insanity.

docs.title: Price types
docs.text:
Type Description One-off Paid once, immediately or at a defined future moment Ongoing Paid continuously for the rest of the petitioner's life Conditional Triggered by a specific future event

Prices are never purely material. The pact system is not a transaction — it is a karmic weight. Prices that seem material (gold, land, an object) always carry a narrative cost that outlasts the payment.

docs.title: Example pact
docs.text:
Offering: You will survive the battle. Price: None of your companions will. Binding: The petitioner survives. Their companions do not. The petitioner will have to live with the knowledge that they chose this.

Note what this is not: the petitioner does not survive as a prisoner, or survive maimed beyond function, or survive in a way that renders survival meaningless. They survive. The price is the price — not a punishment disguised as one.

docs.title: Pact granters
docs.text:
Pact granters are not named, statted, or fully described in the base rules. They are drawn to despair and they offer what is within their power to offer. What they are, what they want, why they respond — these are setting questions.

What the rules specify:

  • They exist
  • They listen
  • They are honest
  • They are not merciful

Placeholder

Pact granter taxonomy (if any), their limitations, and whether they can be sought out deliberately to be developed for the default setting.

docs.title: Kai intersection
docs.text:
A spellcaster who has overdrawn their mana to zero is in precisely the condition that draws pact granters. A pact that offers one final overdraw — at permanent kai cost as the price — is structurally coherent with both systems.

This means kai degradation from overdraw and pact prices can compound. A character who makes a pact to survive by burning their kai is not just spending a resource — they are becoming less of what they were, permanently, knowingly.

docs.title: Cheating a pact
docs.text:
Characters who attempt to find loopholes, technically comply while violating intent, or otherwise cheat the terms of a pact are driven to insanity. This is not a GM ruling. It is a property of the binding.

The insanity is not a mechanical status effect. It is a narrative consequence that the GM adjudicates — but its direction is always toward the spirit of the original price. The universe does not forget what was agreed.

Warning

This mechanic exists to enforce the design principle that tragedy comes from known prices, not betrayal. If the GM is using pact loopholes to punish players, the mechanic is being misused.
